g.title: Attach Feet, Sides, and Bases
g.desc: This step involves attaching 'gummy feet' and black plastic 'side pieces' to two plywood base pieces, then connecting the bases and adding a blue component. First, find two rectangular plywood base pieces (one larger than the other) and eight small, round, soft 'gummy feet'. Turn both base pieces over to their undersides. Attach one gummy foot to each of the four corners of both bases. Next, locate the two long, flat, black plastic 'side pieces', noting their different lengths. For the larger plywood base, align the longer black side piece underneath, matching its three holes. Insert three brass screws (type C, #4-40) from the bottom up through the black piece and plywood, securing with a small, hexagonal brass nut on the top side of the plywood. Repeat this for the smaller plywood base using the shorter black side piece, aligning its two holes, and securing with two screws and nuts in the same manner. Then, connect the two plywood base pieces: the small base has a rectangular tab on one side; the large base has a matching slot. Slide the tab into the slot. Finally, place the small, rectangular, light blue plastic component onto the top of the connected small plywood base, aligning its two holes. Insert two brass screws (type C) from the top, through the blue piece and plywood, securing with nuts from the bottom.

g.title: Tighten Screw and Nut Assembly
g.desc: This drawing shows how to securely fasten parts using a brass screw and a brass nut. First, identify your brass screw. It has a flat, round head at one end and a long, ridged shaft, called threads, on the other. Next, find a small, six-sided brass nut with a threaded hole in its center. To assemble, orient the screw so its flat head is below the piece you are fastening, and its threaded shaft is pointing upwards. Then, place the nut above the piece, lining up its central hole with the screw's shaft. Carefully turn the screw clockwise to thread it into the nut. Keep turning until the screw is fully tightened and the nut feels snug, just as the orange arrow shows the screw moving up into the nut.

g.title: Attach Sensor to Side Piece
g.desc: This step shows how to attach a sensor to one of the main side pieces. First, find the large, flat, rectangular 'side piece' made of 5mm thick plywood. Feel its rounded corners and locate two large rectangular cutouts in the center. Just above these cutouts, you will find two small circular holes. Next, find the long, thin, black plastic 'sensor piece'. At one end, feel for a small, raised electronic component with parallel lines – this is the actual sensor. To assemble, place the plywood side piece flat on a surface. Position the black sensor piece on top of the plywood piece, making sure the raised sensor component is on your right. Align the two holes in the sensor piece with the two small circular holes on the plywood side piece (the ones located above the large central cutouts). Take two 'A' hardware pieces, which are #4-40 brass pan-head screws (feel for a rounded, slightly domed head and threaded shaft). Insert one screw through each aligned hole, starting from the top of the sensor piece and going down into the plywood side piece, then tighten them securely.

g.title: Assemble Frame and IR Sensor
g.desc:
This step builds the main support structure and adds the IR sensor. First, find two identical, curved 'Arm' pieces. They are long, L-shaped pieces with a larger hole near one end and smaller holes along their curve. Next, locate the flat, rectangular 'Ramp' piece, which has a large, open cutout in its center and several smaller rectangular slots. Place the 'Ramp' piece flat, then position one 'Arm' piece on each side of it. The 'Ramp' piece should be nestled *between* the two 'Arms'. Align the holes at the narrower end of each 'Arm' with the corresponding holes on the 'Ramp' piece. Secure these connections using #4-40 brass screws and nuts.
Next, take this assembled 'Arm-Ramp' structure and place it onto the 'Large Base' piece. The 'Large Base' is the broadest, flat rectangular piece with many slots and holes. Align the remaining holes on the 'Arm' pieces with the holes on the 'Large Base' piece. Fasten these connections with more #4-40 brass screws and nuts. Make sure you do not overtighten any screws.
Finally, find the 'IR Beam Sensor'. This is a small, black rectangular plastic component with a red and black wire attached, ending in a small plastic connector. Locate the matching rectangular opening in the 'Large Base' piece. Gently insert the 'IR Beam Sensor' into this opening. Then, connect the plastic connector on the red and black wire to the small pins sticking up from the sensor's circuit board.

text.tspan: Connect both Arm pieces to the
Ramp piece with Screws and Nuts

g.title: Attach Side Panels and Wire Sensor
g.desc: This step guides you in attaching two black plastic side panels, a blue inner piece, and an electronic sensor with its wiring to the main U-shaped wooden structure. First, locate the U-shaped wooden structure, made of 5mm thick plywood. Next, find the two identical, smooth, black rectangular plastic side panels, which are thinner than the plywood. Position one black panel against the *inner* flat surface of each 'arm' of the U-shaped wooden structure. Ensure their holes align with the holes in the wood. These panels are typically fastened using #4-40 brass hardware, though the hardware isn't shown in this diagram. Then, find the flat, light blue rectangular plastic piece, which is thinner than the black panels. This piece fits into a rectangular slot on the *bottom* of the U-shaped wooden structure, between the two black side panels. Feel for the slot and gently press the blue piece into place until it is flush. Now, locate the small, white rectangular electronic sensor, which has two thin wires (one red, one brown) extending from one end. Find the small, rectangular opening on the *outer* side of the *right* black plastic side panel. Carefully push the white sensor into this opening from the outside until it sits firmly, with its wires pointing *inside* the assembly. Finally, take the white plastic connector with multiple colored wires (black, red, yellow, white). Connect the red wire from the sensor to the red wire on this multi-colored connector, and the brown wire from the sensor to the black wire on the connector. For more detailed instructions on all wiring, please refer to the Piper Make tutorials.

g.title: Insert Slope into Ramp and Base Slots
g.desc: First, identify the 'Slope' piece. This is a long, 5mm thick laser-cut plywood piece shaped like an arm or lever, wider at one end and tapering towards the other. Use your fingers to find a rectangular cutout and a small circular hole near its wider, upper end. The narrower, lower end has a specially shaped tab designed to fit into a slot. Next, locate the 'Ramp' piece, which is a long, flat, 5mm thick plywood piece with several small circular holes along one of its long edges. This 'Ramp' piece should be resting on or connected to a larger, flat 'Base' piece, also 5mm thick plywood. Find the rectangular slot on the 'Ramp' piece, usually at one end, and feel for the corresponding, aligned slot on the 'Base' piece directly underneath it. Orient the 'Slope' piece so its wider end points upwards and slightly to your left, and its narrower, tabbed end points downwards and to your right. Carefully align the tabbed end of the 'Slope' piece with the combined slots on the 'Ramp' and 'Base'. Gently push the 'Slope' piece downwards into these aligned slots. The 'Slope' should slide in until it is securely seated, forming an upward angle from the 'Ramp' and 'Base'.

g.title: Assembly Complete: Next Steps
g.desc: This image signifies the successful completion of the kit's physical assembly. The drawing depicts the fully built mechanism, appearing as a stapler-like device or a launcher. Tactilely, you should now have a stable, integrated unit with a flat base, a main body, and a lever arm that pivots. An orange-colored ramp piece is shown along the top of the lever, and a black rectangular component is inserted at the back, likely forming a loading or feeding mechanism. All laser-cut plywood pieces are now fastened together with #4-40 brass hardware, forming a complete and robust structure. The instruction 'ASSEMBLY COMPLETE!' means you've finished building. You can now visit 'make.playpiper.com' to continue your mission.

g.title: Complete Ramp Assembly
g.desc: This image shows the fully assembled ramp structure. First, locate the long, smooth, orange ramp piece. It feels like a wide, shallow U-shaped channel, with raised edges along its length. One end is higher and slightly wider, while the other end is lower and slightly narrower. The higher end of the ramp rests on a sturdy stand, built from several interlocking 5mm thick laser-cut plywood pieces. The lower end of the ramp fits into a smaller, open-topped box-like structure, also made from 5mm plywood. Finally, two identical, flat, rectangular black plastic pieces, which are thinner than the plywood, are attached vertically to the outer sides of the ramp, roughly in the middle. These plastic pieces have two small holes each for #4-40 brass screws. All the plywood and plastic components are connected using #4-40 brass hardware through the designated holes to create this complete ramp system.

g.title: Kit Parts Inventory List
g.desc: This page lists all the parts included in your kit. Carefully feel each item to confirm you have everything. You'll find many 5mm thick laser-cut plywood pieces: a large 'Large Base', a smaller 'Small Base', and pairs of identical 'Side Pieces' and 'Arm Pieces'. There are also plywood 'Ramp' and 'Slope' pieces, which have an incline. Look for two plywood 'Protractors' with curved edges, and four soft, sticky 'Gummy Feet'. Electronic parts include two 'IR Beam Sensors' with wires, and two longer 'Plastic Tracks'. You also have brass #4-40 hardware: eight 1/2-inch and four 1/4-inch 'Pan-Head Screws' with rounded tops, two 'Flat-Head Screws', and eight six-sided 'Hex Nuts'. No assembly or disassembly is shown here; this step is just to check your kit contents.

g.title: Attach Track Connector, Spacer, and Base
g.desc: Step 2: You will need the 'Small Base', 'Track Spacer', 'Track Connector', and two 'B' flat-head screws. First, find the 'Small Base' piece; it's a flat plywood rectangle. Feel its edges: one long side has a U-shaped cut-out in the middle, and the opposite long side has two smaller, rectangular notches, one near each corner. It also has two small circular holes in its center. Next, find two identical flat plywood rectangular pieces with rounded corners, each having two small circular holes near its ends. One is the 'Track Spacer' and the other is the 'Track Connector'. Now, place the 'Small Base' flat on your table. Place the 'Track Spacer' directly on top of the 'Small Base', aligning their two holes. Then, place the 'Track Connector' on top of the 'Track Spacer', aligning all three sets of holes. Finally, take the two 'B' flat-head screws (these have a flat top) and insert one screw down through each set of aligned holes. Use a screwdriver to tighten both screws until all three plywood pieces are securely fastened together.

g.title: Attach Protractor Indicator to Ramp Base
g.desc: Locate the main ramp assembly you are working on. Feel along the edge of one of the flat plywood pieces near where the ramp pivots. You should find a small, rectangular wooden peg sticking straight out. Now, find the protractor indicator piece. This is a flat, narrow piece of 5mm thick plywood. One end is rounded, like a half-circle, with tiny raised lines or bumps along its curved edge – these are the degree markings. This rounded end also has a small, circular hole in its center. The other end is a rectangular handle, and in its middle, it has a long, thin slot cut all the way through. Hold the protractor indicator so the rounded, marked end is pointing away from the rectangular handle. Carefully align the slot in the protractor's rectangular handle with the rectangular wooden peg on the ramp assembly. Slide the protractor indicator straight onto the peg until it is fully seated and feels secure.

g.title: Slide Plastic Track into Connector
g.desc: For step 6, find the orange plastic track. It feels smooth and U-shaped, like a small gutter. One end has a narrower opening and a small bundle of thin, flexible wires attached. Now, locate the black plastic track connector, which is firmly attached to the main plywood structure. Feel for its slot opening. Align the narrower opening of the orange track, with the wires, to the slot on the black connector. Gently slide the plastic track straight into the connector slot until it feels secure and doesn't pull out easily. Make sure the wires are not bent or trapped.
